comprehensive_summary:
Italo Calvino (Italo Giovanni Calvino Mameli) was an Italian postmodernist author associated with the Oulipo literary group, known for their strict mathematical writing constraints. He used mathematics to sequence many of his works.

Cosmicomics is his most frequently clued collection, narrated by the immortal primordial being Qfwfq (spelled Q-F-W-F-Q). "The Distance of the Moon" is the opening and most clued story, set during a time when the moon was so close to Earth that people climbed ladders to it to collect moon-milk. The narrator's deaf cousin (the Deaf One) is the best at climbing and extracting milk from the moon's scales. Captain Vhd Vhd's wife (Mrs. Vhd Vhd) gets trapped on the moon; she stays because she loves the Deaf One, who prods the moon away with a pole. The story ends with Mrs. Vhd Vhd playing the harp on the Moon. A love triangle develops between Qfwfq, the Captain's wife, and the deaf cousin. In "Without Colors," the world gains color for the first time, and Qfwfq's love interest Ayl retreats into the earth. In "All at One Point," life was "packed like sardines" in a singularity before the Big Bang, which happens when Mrs. Ph(i)Nk0 offers to make noodles for everyone. In "The Form of Space," Qfwfq and Lieutenant Fenimore both love Ursula as they travel on parallel paths. In "The Light-Years," the narrator responds "WHAT OF IT?" to a sign saying "I SAW YOU" and creates signs to draw attention to good and bad actions. In "How Much Shall We Bet?," characters bet atoms on far-future events. The collection's stories open with scientific epigraphs. The companion collection t zero includes the title story about a hunter frozen in the moment his arrow flies toward a pouncing lion.

If on a winter's night a traveler is Calvino's celebrated second-person metafictional novel. It opens "You are about to begin reading Italo Calvino's new novel." The protagonist ("you, the Reader") encounters Ludmilla at a bookstore after buying a misprinted copy of Calvino's book. The odd-numbered chapters are written in second person about the Reader's experience, while the even-numbered chapters present excerpts from various novels that cut off at the climax. The writer Silas Flannery is translated by the chaos-inducing Ermes Marana, who introduces bizarre errors. The Organization of Apocryphal Power is interested in Flannery's works. The fictional countries Cimmeria (near the Gulf of Bothnia) and the Cimbrian People's Republic appear. Professor Uzzi-Tuzii teaches Cimmerian literature. The Reader eventually marries Ludmilla (whose sister is Lotaria). "Zeno of Elea" is used as a codeword during a luggage exchange in the first chapter. A character accidentally has sex with Madame Miyagi while pursuing her daughter Makiko.

Invisible Cities is framed as a conversation between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan, in which Polo describes 55 cities named after women (Isidora, Diomira, Dorothea, Ersilia, Despina, Isaura). The cities are described as entities that "like dreams, are made of desires and fears." Khan wonders if his empire is "nothing but a zodiac of the mind's phantasm" while playing chess. Polo uses chess pieces to describe the cities. The novel ends with the line about the "inferno of the living" being "what is already here." Section titles follow a sine wave pattern. Calvino patterned this after Oulipo's mathematical constraints.

The Our Ancestors trilogy consists of: The Cloven Viscount (Medardo of Terralba is split into good and evil halves by a cannonball), The Baron in the Trees (Cosimo refuses to eat a dinner of snails prepared by his sister Battista, flees society by living in trees, hunts with his dachshund Ottimo Massimo; he hangs on a hot-air balloon at the end), and The Nonexistent Knight (the crusader Agilulf is only a set of floating armor serving as Charlemagne's paladin alongside Rambaldo).

Mr. Palomar opens with the title character trying to fully "see" a wave from a beach; he is later overcome realizing his front lawn is infinite. It is a philosophical novel divided into three sections, each with three subsections, each with three chapters.

The Castle of Crossed Destinies has its plot created by characters communicating with tarot cards.

Six Memos for the Next Millennium consists of Harvard lectures (Charles Eliot Norton lectures) about lightness, quickness, exactitude, visibility, and multiplicity. The "Lightness" lecture tells the story of Guido Cavalcanti leaping over his foes. The "Multiplicity" chapter discusses Flaubert's Bouvard and Pecuchet.

"Why Read the Classics?" proposes 14 definitions of classic books, describes them as texts "which constitute a treasured experience" and as books that "exert a peculiar influence." He praises Candide's "narrative rapidity" and Borges's analysis of Ugolino's cannibalism in Dante.

Other works include "Under the Jaguar Sun" (Olivia obsesses over Aztec sacrifice; represents the sense of taste in an unfinished collection about the five senses), "The King Listens" (a paranoid king listens for rebellion), Italian Folktales (1956 collection of 200 folktales including "The Little Girl Sold with the Pears"), The Path to the Nest of Spiders (debut novel about orphaned cobbler's apprentice Pin and partisans in WWII), Marcovaldo, and "The Burning of the Abominable House" (Oulipo-inspired). He called for books that "take a form equivalent to the universe."

works.name: Cosmicomics / t zero
works.description: Collections narrated by the immortal primordial being Qfwfq. Stories open with scientific epigraphs. "The Distance of the Moon" is the most clued story, about collecting milk from the moon via ladders.

clues.clue: "The Distance of the Moon": people climb ladders to collect milk from the moon; the Deaf One is the best at it; Mrs. Vhd Vhd gets trapped on the moon
examples: Captain Vhd Vhd's efforts to collect milk from the moon in a story that features the immortal Qfwfq.
Characters climb a ladder to farm milk from the moon, where Captain Vhd Vhd's wife becomes trapped.

clues.clue: Qfwfq (Q-F-W-F-Q) is the immortal primordial narrator
examples: The hard-to-pronounce Qfwfq narrates many of the stories.
A character whose name is spelled 'Q-F-W-F-Q' is the protagonist.

clues.clue: "Without Colors": Qfwfq falls for Ayl; she retreats into the earth when the world gains color
examples: The main character's love interest runs away after the world gains color.
Qfwfq falls for Ayl while the two are living on a prehistoric version of Earth before colors appear.

clues.clue: "All at One Point": life was "packed like sardines" in a singularity; Big Bang happens when Mrs. Ph(i)Nk0 offers to make noodles
examples: People 'packed like sardines' inside a singularity.
The Big Bang happens when Mrs. Ph(i)Nk0 offers to make noodles for everyone.

clues.clue: "The Form of Space": Qfwfq and Lieutenant Fenimore love Ursula on parallel paths that cannot cross
examples: The narrator and Lieutenant Fenimore both love Ursula, even though their trajectories cannot cross.

clues.clue: "The Light-Years": the narrator responds "WHAT OF IT?" to a sign saying "I SAW YOU"
examples: A character uses a fake finger to point out the better moments of his life in response to a sign saying 'I SAW YOU.'
The narrator replies 'WHAT OF IT?' to a sign saying 'I SAW YOU.'

clues.clue: t zero title story: a hunter frozen in the moment his arrow flies toward a pouncing lion
examples: The title story centers on a hunter waiting to see if his arrow will hit a pouncing lion.
The narrator contemplates if his arrow will hit a lion lunging at him while frozen in time.

clues.clue: "How Much Shall We Bet?": characters bet atoms on far-future events like Assyria invading Mesopotamia
examples: Two characters bet atoms on events that will happen in the far future.
That character bets with the Dean on whether Assyria will invade Mesopotamia.

clues.clue: Stories open with scientific epigraphs/statements
examples: Many tales in another of his collections open with scientific epigraphs.
A scientific statement precedes each story.

clues.clue: "Priscilla": a unicellular narrator evolves into a camel and a primordial sea
examples: A unicellular narrator who evolves into a camel and a primordial sea in a story titled for their love interest, 'Priscilla.'

clues.clue: Second-person novel; odd-numbered chapters about you, the Reader; even-numbered chapters are novel excerpts that cut off at the climax
examples: The odd-numbered chapters are written in the second person and are about the reader's attempts to read the novel itself.
That novel centers around several misprinted books read by Ludmilla and you, the protagonist.

clues.clue: The Reader encounters Ludmilla at a bookstore; eventually marries her; her sister is Lotaria
examples: The protagonist encounters Ludmilla at a bookstore.
You fall in love with Ludmilla in this author's novel.

clues.clue: The writer Silas Flannery is translated by the chaos-inducing Ermes Marana; the Organization of Apocryphal Power
examples: The writer Silas Flannery is translated by the chaos-inducing Ermes Marana.

clues.clue: The fictional countries Cimmeria (near Gulf of Bothnia) and the Cimbrian People's Republic
examples: A novel by this author contains a description of the fictional country of Cimmeria, which is located somewhere near the Gulf of Bothnia.
This novel features a debate by professors of Cimmerian and Cimbrian literature.

examples: The protagonist encounters the eccentric Professor Uzzi-Tuzii.

works.name: Invisible Cities
works.description: Framed as a conversation between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan. Polo describes 55 cities named after women (Isidora, Diomira, Dorothea, Ersilia, Despina, Isaura). Section titles follow a sine wave pattern reflecting Oulipo constraints.

clues.clue: Framed as conversations between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan; Polo describes 55 cities named after women
examples: This author wrote a novel framed by a conversation between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan.
The title entities are described to Kubla Khan by Marco Polo.

clues.clue: Cities include Isidora, Diomira, Dorothea, Ersilia, Despina, Isaura
examples: Isidora, Diomira, and Ersilia are among the title places.
Polo describes locations like Diomira, sorted into categories such as 'Thin,' 'Dead,' 'Continuous,' and 'Hidden.'

clues.clue: Cities are "like dreams, made of desires and fears"; Khan wonders if his empire is "nothing but a zodiac of the mind's phantasm"
examples: Entities that 'like dreams, are made of desires and fears.'
A ruler wonders if his empire is 'nothing but a zodiac of the mind's phantasm' while playing chess.

clues.clue: Ends with the "inferno of the living" being "what is already here"
examples: A novel by this author ends as a character explains that the 'inferno of the living is not something that will be... it is what is already here.'

clues.clue: Polo uses chess pieces to describe the cities
examples: An explorer uses chess pieces to describe the title constructs.

clues.clue: Coloured strings between houses represent relationships in Ersilia
examples: This writer's work describes how coloured strings between houses represent relationships in the town of Ersilia.

works.name: Our Ancestors Trilogy
works.description: Three novels: The Cloven Viscount (Medardo split in half by cannonball), The Baron in the Trees (Cosimo lives in trees after refusing to eat snails), and The Nonexistent Knight (Agilulf is only floating armor).

clues.clue: The Baron in the Trees: Cosimo refuses snails prepared by sister Battista; hunts with dachshund Ottimo Massimo; hangs on a hot-air balloon at the end
examples: This author created a boy who hunts small game with his faithful dachshund Ottimo Massimo after fleeing a meal of snails prepared by his sister Battista.
A novel by this author sees Cosimo refuse to eat the dinner of snails.

clues.clue: The Cloven Viscount: Medardo of Terralba split into good and evil halves by a cannonball
examples: The Medardo of Terralba is divided into 'good' and 'bad' halves.
Medardo of Terralba literally becomes two people, one good and one evil, after a cannonball splits him in half.

clues.clue: The Nonexistent Knight: Agilulf is only a set of floating armor serving Charlemagne; alongside Rambaldo
examples: The crusader Agilulf joins Charlemagne's army despite only being a set of floating armor.
The title animated suit of armor serves as a paladin of Charlemagne alongside the young Rambaldo.

works.name: Six Memos for the Next Millennium / Why Read the Classics?
works.description: Six Memos consists of Harvard lectures on lightness, quickness, exactitude, visibility, and multiplicity. "Why Read the Classics?" proposes 14 definitions of classic books.

clues.clue: Six Memos: lectures on lightness, quickness, exactitude, visibility, multiplicity; "Lightness" tells of Cavalcanti leaping over foes
examples: His books contains essays about quickness, exactitude, visibility, multiplicity, and most notably lightness.
This author related the story of Guido Cavalcanti leaping over the heads of his foes in a lecture declaring 'Lightness' an essential trait.

clues.clue: "Why Read the Classics?": proposes 14 definitions; books that "exert a peculiar influence"; praises Candide's "narrative rapidity"
examples: An essay by this author extols the virtues of books that 'take a form equivalent to the universe.'
This author proposed 14 definitions of the title books in Why Read the Classics?

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Mr. Palomar, The Castle of Crossed Destinies, The Path to the Nest of Spiders, Italian Folktales, "Under the Jaguar Sun," Marcovaldo, and other works.

clues.clue: Mr. Palomar: the title character tries to "see" a wave from a beach; his front lawn is infinite
examples: One novel opens with the title character trying to fully 'see' a wave from a beach.
A philosophical novel divided into three sections, each of which has three subsections, each of which has three chapters.

clues.clue: The Castle of Crossed Destinies: characters communicate with tarot cards
examples: Characters communicate with tarot cards in this author's novel The Castle of Crossed Destinies.

clues.clue: The Path to the Nest of Spiders: debut novel about orphaned cobbler's apprentice Pin and partisans in WWII
examples: The protagonist is an orphaned cobbler's apprentice named Pin, who becomes involved with a partisan group in World War 2.

clues.clue: Italian Folktales: 1956 collection of 200 folktales including "The Little Girl Sold with the Pears"
examples: He included 'The Little Girl Sold with the Pears' in a 1956 collection of 200 of his country's folktales.

clues.clue: "Under the Jaguar Sun": Olivia obsesses over Aztec sacrifice; represents taste in an unfinished five-senses collection
examples: Olivia obsesses over Aztec human sacrifice and whether the dead people would be cannibalized.
That story set here represents the sense of taste in an unfinished Italo Calvino collection.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Italian postmodernist author associated with the Oulipo literary group. Used mathematics to sequence his works. His collaborations with Oulipo inspired the sine wave pattern of Invisible Cities.

clues.clue: Member of the Oulipo literary group; used mathematics to sequence works
examples: This author's collaborations with the Oulipo group inspired him to use mathematics to sequence many of his works.
Calvino patterned section titles in Invisible Cities after a sine wave, signifying his membership in Oulipo.
